aliases: Deep Fried Mushroom in Chilli Sauce

english: Deep Fried Mushroom in Chilli Sauce

food_subgroup: Mushroom dishes, deep-fried with chilli sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Indo-Chinese chilli mushroom; closest parent 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03302114) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Mushrooms and other fungi, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code for Indo-Chinese chilli mushroom preparation exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Indo-Chinese mushroom dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of chilli mushroom is approximately 200 g, comprising deep-fried mushroom pieces tossed in a spiced chilli-soy sauce, served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom dishes, cooked — estimated from USDA FoodData Central (button mushrooms, cooked) and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (fried vegetable preparations with sauce)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese chilli mushroom exists in any approved primary source. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked button mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) with ICMR-NIN 2017 data for deep-fried vegetable preparations and Indo-Chinese sauce components (soy sauce, chilli, cornflour batter, oil). Deep-frying oil absorption estimated at ~8–10 g fat per 100 g finished product. Sodium elevated due to soy sauce. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for a restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable dishes with sauce — analogous estimate based on stir-fried/deep-fried vegetable preparations with cornflour and soy sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indo-Chinese chilli mushroom. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous deep-fried vegetable preparations with cornflour batter and sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database. Mushrooms have a very low intrinsic GI; cornflour batter and soy sauce moderately elevate the effective GI. GL per 200 g serving = (45 × 12.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 11.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid chilli mushroom due to its high sodium load from soy sauce; a single restaurant serving can contribute over 40% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from cornflour batter and sauce sugars; low GI but portion size matters
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chilli mushroom has a low GI (~45) but a moderate glycemic load per serving (~11.5 g); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume moderate portions and be aware of the added sugars and refined starch in the batter and sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat from deep-frying; low saturated fat but high total fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) and deep-frying fat (~9.5 g/100 g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Mushroom is a popular Indo-Chinese dish widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia. It consists of button mushroom pieces coated in a seasoned cornflour batter, deep-fried until crispy, and then tossed in a bold sauce made from chilli, soy sauce, vinegar, garlic, ginger, green capsicum, and onion. The dish is available in two variants: dry (where the sauce clings to the mushrooms with minimal liquid) and gravy (where a thicker, saucier consistency is achieved). The texture is crispy on the outside and tender inside, with a flavour profile that is spicy, tangy, savoury, and slightly sweet. Nutritionally, chilli mushroom provides moderate energy (~148 kcal/100 g), meaningful protein (~4.2 g/100 g) from mushrooms, and notable fat (~9.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying. Sodium is elevated (~520 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~11.5 g). It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly. Clinically, it is best consumed in moderation by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli mushroom?
What is the difference between dry and gravy chilli mushroom?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese chilli mushroom exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA cooked mushroom data with ICMR-NIN fried vegetable preparation data and standard sauce component contributions. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level estimation is grounded in approved sources and the preparation method is well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from approved component sources (USDA FoodData Central cooked button mushrooms FDC ID 169251; ICMR-NIN 2017 fried vegetable preparations). No single approved source entry covers this exact dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as mushrooms and plant-based sauce ingredients contain no dietary cholesterol; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for mushroom-based preparations. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked mushroom and sauce component data; no -1 sentinel values are used as plausible estimates are available. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable deep-fried vegetable preparations with cornflour batter; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 6. The dish is served at Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine (Boat Quay, Singapore) at SGD 16.90. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 8. FoodOn match type is closest_parent; FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent; SNOMED CT match type is none — all documented with gap notes.
